replies.title: Show All Students
explanation: SELECT chooses columns.
* means all columns.
FROM students tells SQL which table to read.
replies.dry_run: Run the setup SQL first, then run only the final query. Read the table names and column names carefully before writing the query.
common_mistakes: Forgetting FROM.
Writing the table name before SELECT.
Missing the semicolon in some editors.
replies.final_code:
-- Common setup for today's SQL questions
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS enrollments;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS courses;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS students;

CREATE TABLE students (
    student_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT,
    city TEXT,
    marks INTEGER,
    fees_paid INTEGER
);

INSERT INTO students (student_id, name, city, marks, fees_paid) VALUES
(1, 'Asha', 'Varanasi', 92, 1),
(2, 'Ravi', 'Lucknow', 76, 1),
(3, 'Meera', 'Varanasi', 88, 0),
(4, 'Kabir', 'Patna', 65, 1),
(5, 'Neha', 'Lucknow', 95, 0),
(6, 'Ishan', 'Delhi', 72, 1),
(7, 'Tara', 'Varanasi', 81, 1);

CREATE TABLE courses (
    course_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    course_name TEXT,
    fee INTEGER
);

INSERT INTO courses (course_id, course_name, fee) VALUES
(101, 'Python', 3000),
(102, 'SQL', 2500),
(103, 'Data Structures', 4000);

CREATE TABLE enrollments (
    enrollment_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    student_id INTEGER,
    course_id INTEGER,
    FOREIGN KEY (student_id) REFERENCES students(student_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (course_id) REFERENCES courses(course_id)
);

INSERT INTO enrollments (enrollment_id, student_id, course_id) VALUES
(1, 1, 101),
(2, 1, 102),
(3, 2, 102),
(4, 3, 101),
(5, 4, 103),
(6, 5, 102),
(7, 6, 103),
(8, 7, 101),
(9, 7, 103);

SELECT * FROM students;

replies.title: Show Only Names and Cities
replies.short_answer: Select the required columns by name.
explanation: SQL allows us to choose only the columns we need.
This keeps results cleaner and easier to read.
replies.dry_run: Run the setup SQL first, then run only the final query. Read the table names and column names carefully before writing the query.
common_mistakes: Using name city without comma.
Using column names that do not exist.

replies.title: Find Students from Varanasi
explanation: WHERE keeps only rows that satisfy a condition.
Text comparisons should use quoted text values.
replies.dry_run: Run the setup SQL first, then run only the final query. Read the table names and column names carefully before writing the query.
common_mistakes: Forgetting quotes around Varanasi.
Using == instead of = in SQL.

replies.title: Find Students Scoring More Than 80
explanation: Numeric columns do not need quotes.
The condition marks > 80 keeps only high-scoring students.
replies.dry_run: Run the setup SQL first, then run only the final query. Read the table names and column names carefully before writing the query.
common_mistakes: Putting 80 in quotes is usually unnecessary.
Writing greater than as => instead of >= or >.

replies.title: Sort Students by Marks
explanation: ORDER BY sorts the result.
DESC means descending, so the highest marks come first.
replies.dry_run: Run the setup SQL first, then run only the final query. Read the table names and column names carefully before writing the query.
common_mistakes: Using SORT BY instead of ORDER BY.
Forgetting DESC and getting lowest marks first.

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS courses;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS students;

CREATE TABLE students (

replies.title: Count Total Students
explanation: Aggregate functions calculate a single result from many rows.
COUNT(*) counts all rows in the table.
replies.dry_run: Run the setup SQL first, then run only the final query. Read the table names and column names carefully before writing the query.
common_mistakes: Using COUNT without parentheses.
Counting a nullable column when COUNT(*) is simpler.
replies.final_code:
-- Common setup for today's SQL questions
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS enrollments;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS courses;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS students;

CREATE TABLE students (
    student_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT,
    city TEXT,
    marks INTEGER,
    fees_paid INTEGER
);

(5, 'Neha', 'Lucknow', 95, 0),
(6, 'Ishan', 'Delhi', 72, 1),
(7, 'Tara', 'Varanasi', 81, 1);

CREATE TABLE courses (
    course_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    course_name TEXT,
    fee INTEGER
);

INSERT INTO courses (course_id, course_name, fee) VALUES
(101, 'Python', 3000),
(102, 'SQL', 2500),
(103, 'Data Structures', 4000);

    enrollment_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,

    course_id INTEGER,
    FOREIGN KEY (student_id) REFERENCES students(student_id),

);

replies.title: Find Average Marks
explanation: AVG calculates the arithmetic average.
It is useful when we need a summary of a numeric column.
replies.dry_run: Run the setup SQL first, then run only the final query. Read the table names and column names carefully before writing the query.
common_mistakes: Using AVERAGE instead of AVG.
Forgetting FROM students.
replies.final_code:
-- Common setup for today's SQL questions
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS enrollments;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS courses;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS students;

CREATE TABLE students (
    student_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT,
    city TEXT,
    marks INTEGER,
    fees_paid INTEGER
);

INSERT INTO students (student_id, name, city, marks, fees_paid) VALUES

    fee INTEGER

INSERT INTO courses (course_id, course_name, fee) VALUES
(101, 'Python', 3000),
(102, 'SQL', 2500),
(103, 'Data Structures', 4000);

    enrollment_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,

(3, 2, 102),
(4, 3, 101),
(5, 4, 103),
(6, 5, 102),
(7, 6, 103),
(8, 7, 101),

replies.title: Count Students City Wise
replies.short_answer: Group rows by city and count each group.
explanation: GROUP BY combines rows with the same city.
COUNT(*) then counts rows inside each city group.
replies.dry_run: Run the setup SQL first, then run only the final query. Read the table names and column names carefully before writing the query.
common_mistakes: Selecting city without GROUP BY city.
Grouping by the wrong column.
replies.final_code:
-- Common setup for today's SQL questions
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS enrollments;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS courses;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS students;

CREATE TABLE students (
    student_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT,
    city TEXT,
    marks INTEGER,
    fees_paid INTEGER
);

INSERT INTO students (student_id, name, city, marks, fees_paid) VALUES
(1, 'Asha', 'Varanasi', 92, 1),
(2, 'Ravi', 'Lucknow', 76, 1),
(3, 'Meera', 'Varanasi', 88, 0),
(4, 'Kabir', 'Patna', 65, 1),
(5, 'Neha', 'Lucknow', 95, 0),
(6, 'Ishan', 'Delhi', 72, 1),
(7, 'Tara', 'Varanasi', 81, 1);

CREATE TABLE courses (
    course_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    course_name TEXT,
    fee INTEGER
);

INSERT INTO courses (course_id, course_name, fee) VALUES
(101, 'Python', 3000),
(102, 'SQL', 2500),
(103, 'Data Structures', 4000);

CREATE TABLE enrollments (
    enrollment_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    student_id INTEGER,
    course_id INTEGER,
    FOREIGN KEY (student_id) REFERENCES students(student_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (course_id) REFERENCES courses(course_id)
);

INSERT INTO enrollments (enrollment_id, student_id, course_id) VALUES
(1, 1, 101),
(2, 1, 102),
(3, 2, 102),
(4, 3, 101),
(5, 4, 103),
(6, 5, 102),
(7, 6, 103),
(8, 7, 101),
(9, 7, 103);

SELECT city, COUNT(*) AS student_count
FROM students
GROUP BY city;

replies.title: Find Cities with More Than One Student
replies.short_answer: Use HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 after GROUP BY.
explanation: HAVING is used when the condition depends on an aggregate result.
Here the aggregate result is COUNT(*).
replies.dry_run: Run the setup SQL first, then run only the final query. Read the table names and column names carefully before writing the query.
common_mistakes: Using WHERE COUNT(*) > 1.
Forgetting GROUP BY.
replies.final_code:
-- Common setup for today's SQL questions
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS enrollments;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS courses;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS students;

CREATE TABLE students (
    student_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT,
    city TEXT,
    marks INTEGER,
    fees_paid INTEGER
);

INSERT INTO students (student_id, name, city, marks, fees_paid) VALUES
(1, 'Asha', 'Varanasi', 92, 1),
(2, 'Ravi', 'Lucknow', 76, 1),
(3, 'Meera', 'Varanasi', 88, 0),
(4, 'Kabir', 'Patna', 65, 1),
(5, 'Neha', 'Lucknow', 95, 0),
(6, 'Ishan', 'Delhi', 72, 1),
(7, 'Tara', 'Varanasi', 81, 1);

CREATE TABLE courses (
    course_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    course_name TEXT,
    fee INTEGER
);

INSERT INTO courses (course_id, course_name, fee) VALUES
(101, 'Python', 3000),
(102, 'SQL', 2500),
(103, 'Data Structures', 4000);

CREATE TABLE enrollments (
    enrollment_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    student_id INTEGER,
    course_id INTEGER,
    FOREIGN KEY (student_id) REFERENCES students(student_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (course_id) REFERENCES courses(course_id)
);

INSERT INTO enrollments (enrollment_id, student_id, course_id) VALUES
(1, 1, 101),
(2, 1, 102),
(3, 2, 102),
(4, 3, 101),
(5, 4, 103),
(6, 5, 102),
(7, 6, 103),
(8, 7, 101),
(9, 7, 103);

SELECT city, COUNT(*) AS student_count
FROM students
GROUP BY city
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

replies.title: Show Student Names with Course Names
replies.short_answer: Join enrollments with students and courses.
explanation: The enrollments table connects students and courses.
JOIN combines related rows from different tables using matching ids.
replies.dry_run: Run the setup SQL first, then run only the final query. Read the table names and column names carefully before writing the query.
common_mistakes: Joining students directly to courses without enrollments.
Forgetting the ON condition.
replies.final_code:
-- Common setup for today's SQL questions
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS enrollments;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS courses;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS students;

CREATE TABLE students (
    student_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT,
    city TEXT,
    marks INTEGER,
    fees_paid INTEGER
);

INSERT INTO students (student_id, name, city, marks, fees_paid) VALUES
(1, 'Asha', 'Varanasi', 92, 1),
(2, 'Ravi', 'Lucknow', 76, 1),
(3, 'Meera', 'Varanasi', 88, 0),
(4, 'Kabir', 'Patna', 65, 1),
(5, 'Neha', 'Lucknow', 95, 0),
(6, 'Ishan', 'Delhi', 72, 1),
(7, 'Tara', 'Varanasi', 81, 1);

CREATE TABLE courses (
    course_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    course_name TEXT,
    fee INTEGER
);

INSERT INTO courses (course_id, course_name, fee) VALUES
(101, 'Python', 3000),
(102, 'SQL', 2500),
(103, 'Data Structures', 4000);

CREATE TABLE enrollments (
    enrollment_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    student_id INTEGER,
    course_id INTEGER,
    FOREIGN KEY (student_id) REFERENCES students(student_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (course_id) REFERENCES courses(course_id)
);

INSERT INTO enrollments (enrollment_id, student_id, course_id) VALUES
(1, 1, 101),
(2, 1, 102),
(3, 2, 102),
(4, 3, 101),
(5, 4, 103),
(6, 5, 102),
(7, 6, 103),
(8, 7, 101),
(9, 7, 103);

SELECT students.name, courses.course_name
FROM enrollments
JOIN students ON enrollments.student_id = students.student_id
JOIN courses ON enrollments.course_id = courses.course_id;

replies.title: Show All Students and Their Courses
replies.short_answer: Use LEFT JOIN starting from students.
explanation: LEFT JOIN keeps all rows from the left table.
This is useful when some students may not have enrollments.
replies.dry_run: Run the setup SQL first, then run only the final query. Read the table names and column names carefully before writing the query.
common_mistakes: Using INNER JOIN and losing students without courses.
Starting from enrollments when the requirement says all students.
replies.final_code:
-- Common setup for today's SQL questions
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS enrollments;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS courses;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS students;

CREATE TABLE students (
    student_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT,
    city TEXT,
    marks INTEGER,
    fees_paid INTEGER
);

INSERT INTO students (student_id, name, city, marks, fees_paid) VALUES
(1, 'Asha', 'Varanasi', 92, 1),
(2, 'Ravi', 'Lucknow', 76, 1),
(3, 'Meera', 'Varanasi', 88, 0),
(4, 'Kabir', 'Patna', 65, 1),
(5, 'Neha', 'Lucknow', 95, 0),
(6, 'Ishan', 'Delhi', 72, 1),
(7, 'Tara', 'Varanasi', 81, 1);

CREATE TABLE courses (
    course_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    course_name TEXT,
    fee INTEGER
);

INSERT INTO courses (course_id, course_name, fee) VALUES
(101, 'Python', 3000),
(102, 'SQL', 2500),
(103, 'Data Structures', 4000);

CREATE TABLE enrollments (
    enrollment_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    student_id INTEGER,
    course_id INTEGER,
    FOREIGN KEY (student_id) REFERENCES students(student_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (course_id) REFERENCES courses(course_id)
);

INSERT INTO enrollments (enrollment_id, student_id, course_id) VALUES
(1, 1, 101),
(2, 1, 102),
(3, 2, 102),
(4, 3, 101),
(5, 4, 103),
(6, 5, 102),
(7, 6, 103),
(8, 7, 101),
(9, 7, 103);

SELECT students.name, courses.course_name
FROM students
LEFT JOIN enrollments ON students.student_id = enrollments.student_id
LEFT JOIN courses ON enrollments.course_id = courses.course_id;

replies.title: Find the Topper
explanation: The inner query finds the highest mark.
The outer query returns students whose marks match that highest value.
This also works if two students share the top score.
replies.dry_run: Run the setup SQL first, then run only the final query. Read the table names and column names carefully before writing the query.
common_mistakes: Using ORDER BY and LIMIT when the question asks for all toppers.
Forgetting parentheses around the subquery.
replies.final_code:
-- Common setup for today's SQL questions
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS enrollments;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS courses;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS students;

CREATE TABLE students (
    student_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT,
    city TEXT,
    marks INTEGER,
    fees_paid INTEGER
);

INSERT INTO students (student_id, name, city, marks, fees_paid) VALUES
(1, 'Asha', 'Varanasi', 92, 1),
(2, 'Ravi', 'Lucknow', 76, 1),
(3, 'Meera', 'Varanasi', 88, 0),
(4, 'Kabir', 'Patna', 65, 1),
(5, 'Neha', 'Lucknow', 95, 0),
(6, 'Ishan', 'Delhi', 72, 1),
(7, 'Tara', 'Varanasi', 81, 1);

CREATE TABLE courses (
    course_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    course_name TEXT,
    fee INTEGER
);

INSERT INTO courses (course_id, course_name, fee) VALUES
(101, 'Python', 3000),
(102, 'SQL', 2500),
(103, 'Data Structures', 4000);

CREATE TABLE enrollments (
    enrollment_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    student_id INTEGER,
    course_id INTEGER,
    FOREIGN KEY (student_id) REFERENCES students(student_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (course_id) REFERENCES courses(course_id)
);

INSERT INTO enrollments (enrollment_id, student_id, course_id) VALUES
(1, 1, 101),
(2, 1, 102),
(3, 2, 102),
(4, 3, 101),
(5, 4, 103),
(6, 5, 102),
(7, 6, 103),
(8, 7, 101),
(9, 7, 103);

SELECT name, marks
FROM students
WHERE marks = (SELECT MAX(marks) FROM students);
